news.title: Fan group launch stinging attack on Fifa over 'World Cup that failed supporters' with 'serious concerns' over next hosts
news.lede: A FOOTBALL fan group has slammed Fifa for the 2026 World Cup and are already worried about what’s to come in 2030. Football Supporters Europe have taken aim at Fifa president Gianni Infantino, US president Donald Trump and Fifa as a whole in a statement. It reads: “The 2026 World Cup should have been a celebration of the game and the supporters who give it life. “Instead, it has too often become a case study in what is so deeply wrong with football today.”

news.title: World Cup changes our readers would make: Ban offsides in the area? Timeouts? Shrink penalty box?
news.lede: Be specific on stoppage time? Replace a red-carded player? Subscribers have their say...

news.title: World Cup 2026 awards: the Guardian team give their final tournament verdicts
news.lede: The best games, goals and personal highlights – from a magical Azteca to Erling Haaland breaking America. There were plenty more games with more goals but none could match the drama of the penalty shootout in this last-32 tie. Germany aren’t supposed to lose in them but they did this time in spectacular fashion. Algeria 3-3 Austria was a completely strange fever dream, seeming to exist on a separate plane of reality.

news.title: World Cup 2030 (very early) power rankings: who will challenge Spain at top?
news.lede: The next men’s World Cup is still four years away, but we take a look at the teams with the firmest foundations who should be primed to contend. The 2026 World Cup provided many an esteemed veteran with what could be their international swansongs, with golden greats Lionel Messi, Cristiano Ronaldo, Luka Modric, Neymar, James Rodríguez and Guillermo Ochoa all making teary exits as their tournament ended. As we turn ahead to the 2030 World Cup, any player 32 or older will be sweating their selection status and changing their daily routines to stay sharp against inevitable aging. For these power rankings, we’re overvaluing sure things.

news.title: Why many Black Americans were rooting for Argentina to lose the World Cup | Etan Thomas
news.lede: Many Black Americans were not cheering Spain’s complicated history. They were rooting against the racism, politics and silence associated with Argentina. There are moments in sports when you realize people aren’t just watching the score any more. They’re watching who speaks.

news.title: Bayern Munich’s Harry Kane defends Thomas Tuchel amid World Cup criticism
news.lede: Since winning the 1966 World Cup in front of a home crowd, England has been chasing an elusive second trophy. Heading into the 2026 tournament, England held the third-best odds behind eventual winner, Spain, and fourth-place finisher, France. Bayern Munich superstar Harry Kane wore the captain’s armband with pride but the English squad fell short of the ultimate goal due to a late collapse against Argentina in the semifinals. England manager Thomas Tuchel received the (three) lions’ share of the criticism for the defeat because he opted to drop into a deep shell with his tactical and substitution decisions.

news.title: Mauro: World Cup final shows why Italian football is struggling
news.lede: Massimo Mauro has explained that a lack of Serie A talent in the 2026 World Cup final is an indicator of where Italian football sits in the global stage, and says that several high-profile clubs need to pull their fingers out in the transfer window if they are to avoid having difficult 2026-27 seasons. The start of the 2026-27 Serie A season is now exactly one month away. Despite the absence of the Italy national team, it has been a busy summer of World Cup action, but former Juventus midfielder Mauro believes that the tournament in North America did not present Italian football, at club or international level, in the best light.

news.title: World Cup becoming a 64-team tournament would create logistical challenges - so can Gianni Infantino's idea work?
news.lede: This year's World Cup is finally over - and after 48 teams took part, it was a long, long tournament. But just as we bid to say goodbye to the 2026 edition, this competition may be getting even bigger. FIFA president Gianni Infantino says any further expansion of the World Cup for 2030 - where six countries are set to host the tournament - ‌will be discussed now the 2026 World Cup has finished. And one solution FIFA are discussing is a 64-team tournament.
